The issue is the downtown West Orange alliance, which has become another black eye of West Orange. There have been so many questions and accusations and what I call excuse me damage control that in order to educate myself and fully understand the purpose for the creation of the alliance, its goals and objectives, its responsib

ilities, its compliance with documented practices and procedures, and its overall performance. I did this by reviewing as many documents as I could get my hands on. It wasn't easy to get the information and there's still more I'm hoping to get from the interim executive director of the alliance. Specifically, I reviewed the ordinance creating the special improvement district known as the SID, the bylaws of the Downtown West Orange Alliance, the 2010 2011 audits of the alliance performed by Peterson and Company, and the 2013 Alliance budget and narratives and state statute 4055D-3. My research revealed that the contention by certain members of the township council that the alliance is an independent body and the council has no control over it is not true. And I base that on the following documented facts. Number one, section 13 of the SID ordinance titled no limitations of powers states that the township council by ordinance can abandon the SID, amend the description of the SID, and at any time enact, change or repeal any rule or 648

regulation adopted on the operation of the SID. Number two, article 3, section one of the alliance bylaws titled Board of Trustees states, "The board of trustees shall consist of 11 voting members made up of one township council member who shall be appointed by the majority vote of township council members." Mr. Gino is that council member, and he did vote for Miss Bril in what is still a questionable election. Each council member and the mayor appoint one member each. This adds up to seven of the 11 voting members, which is a controlling number of votes. Nowhere in the SID ordinance is there any mention of an executive director. The only place it's mentioned is in the alliance bylaws, and there it's mentioned only once in article 3, section two titled powers, which is under the board of trustees. It states, quote, "The board also may hire or retain an executive director to carry out the day-to-day business affairs of the corporation." This then raises the question, does the alliance really need an exe

cutive director? The use of the words may and shall could be the deciding factor. According to state statute 4055D-3, which states the term shall indicates a mandatory requirement and the term may indicates a permissive action. The bylaws clearly state may.
